title: The Role of Audio in Game Feel
html:
Why audio is half of game feel

Game feel is the felt sense of control and responsiveness a game gives you, and audio carries far more of it than most people realize. When a button click has a crisp sound, a jump has a satisfying whoomph, and a hit lands with a meaty thud, the game feels tactile even though you are only touching a controller. Mute a great game and it instantly feels lifeless. Audio is the fastest, cheapest way to make actions feel like they matter.

Audio in games does three jobs at once: it provides feedback (confirming an action happened), information (telling you where an enemy is, that your health is low, that a quest updated), and emotion (tension, triumph, dread, calm). A single gunshot sound confirms you fired, tells nearby players your direction, and contributes to the visceral mood of combat.

The four audio layers

Almost every game soundscape decomposes into four layers. Learning to hear them separately is the core skill of an audio designer.

LayerWhat it isExamples
UIMenu and HUD feedbackButton clicks, hovers, error beeps, level-up chimes
SFXDiegetic gameplay soundsFootsteps, weapons, doors, pickups, impacts
AmbienceThe continuous bed of the worldWind, rain, crowd murmur, machine hum, birds
MusicScored, often non-diegetic emotionLoops, stingers, adaptive layers

Diegetic sounds exist in the game world (a character can hear them); non-diegetic sounds are for the player only (most music, UI). Knowing which is which guides spatialization and mixing decisions later.

Feedback timing and the 100ms rule

Feedback that arrives late feels broken. Aim for sound to trigger within roughly 100 milliseconds of the action. Latency beyond that breaks the cause-and-effect illusion. This is why UI clicks fire on press, not on release, and why footstep sounds are tied to the exact frame a foot plants.

Tip.
When a game feels mushy or unresponsive, check audio latency before you blame the controls. A 200ms delay on hit-confirmation sounds will make perfectly tuned combat feel sluggish.

Auditing a soundscape critically

To analyze any clip, listen four times, once for each layer, ignoring the others. Ask of each sound: Why is it here? What does it tell me? Could the player function without it? Good audio is intentional. Bad audio is either missing (silence where feedback should be) or cluttered (everything loud, nothing readable).

audio-audit-checklist
# For each gameplay moment, ask: UI Does every interaction give feedback within 100ms? SFX Does each action have a distinct, readable sound? Ambience Is there a continuous bed selling the location? Music Does it match emotional intensity and adapt? Mix Can you hear the important sound over everything?

Writing an audio brief

Before making a single sound, list what the game needs. An audio brief is a spreadsheet-like inventory: every event that needs a sound, its layer, its mood, and a reference. This prevents the classic mistake of polishing one cool sound while the game has fifty silent events.

Lab note.
A brief column for variation count matters: footsteps with one sample sound robotic. Plan 3-5 variations for any frequently repeated sound from the start.
🎮 Build This: Audit & Brief

Map the audio of a real game, then plan the audio for your own prototype.

  1. Pick a 60-second gameplay clip of any game you know well.
  2. Watch it four times, once per layer (UI, SFX, ambience, music). Write down every sound you hear in that layer.
  3. For each, note: diegetic or not, what it communicates, and whether it could be removed.
  4. Now make an audio brief for a simple prototype (say, a top-down explorer). List every event needing sound: footsteps, UI click, item pickup, door, ambient wind, one music loop.
  5. Add columns for layer, mood (1-3 words), reference sound, and variation count.
  6. You now have a shopping list that drives every following lesson.

title: Sourcing and Creating Sound Effects: Recording, Foley, Libraries, and Synthesis
html:
Four ways to get a sound

Every SFX you will ever use comes from one of four sources: you record the real thing, you perform foley (fake the real thing with props), you pull from a library of pre-made sounds, or you synthesize it from scratch. Real projects mix all four. The art is picking the right source for each sound.

Field recording

Recording real-world sound gives unmatched authenticity. You do not need a film rig: a modern phone records usable audio, and a cheap USB or handheld recorder (Zoom H1n class) is a huge upgrade. The rules that matter most:

  • Get close. Proximity beats expensive gear. Microphones capture the room as much as the source, so reduce the distance to reduce unwanted reverb.
  • Record quiet. Background hum, traffic, and fridge noise are nearly impossible to remove cleanly. Find the quietest moment and place.
  • Set levels with headroom. Aim for peaks around -12 dB. Digital clipping (going over 0 dB) is unrecoverable distortion.
  • Record many takes. You want variations, and most takes have a cough, a click, or a bump you will discard.

Foley

Foley is performing sound with surrogate props. Footsteps are recorded by a person stepping in a box of gravel or on a wood board. Classic substitutions that sound better than the real thing:

Game soundFoley prop
Footsteps on snowSqueezing a bag of cornstarch
Fire crackleCrumpling cellophane / crushing a chip bag
Bone break / punchSnapping celery, hitting raw meat
Leather creak / saddleTwisting an old wallet or belt
Tip.
Foley a footstep by recording on the actual surface the prop suggests. Step deliberately and record at least eight individual steps so you have variations to layer later.

Free libraries

You do not have to make everything. High-quality free sources include Freesound.org (community uploads, check licenses), BBC Sound Effects archive, and bundled engine libraries. Always verify the license: many require attribution (Creative Commons BY) and some forbid commercial use. Keep a record of every clip's source and license in your project.

Licensing.
Using a sound without checking its license can force you to pull a shipped game. Save the URL, author, and license text for every downloaded asset. CC0 means no attribution required; CC-BY requires credit; never assume.

Synthesis

Synthetic sounds are generated from oscillators and noise rather than recordings. They are ideal for UI blips, sci-fi effects, lasers, and retro game sounds because they are clean, tiny, and infinitely tweakable. A basic UI blip is a short sine or square tone with a fast attack and a quick decay envelope. Free synth tools include the synth built into any DAW, the web-based Bfxr/Sfxr (perfect for arcade-style blips and pickups), and Audacity's Generate menu.

synthesize a UI blip in Audacity
# A simple confirmation blip from a tone + envelope 1. Generate > Tone: Sine, frequency 880 Hz, duration 0.12s 2. Select the clip, Effect > Fade Out # gives a percussive decay 3. Generate a second tone at 1320 Hz on a new track 4. Offset it ~20ms later for a two-note "ding" 5. Export > blip_confirm.wav
🎮 Build This: Three Sounds, Three Sources

Build a tiny SFX palette using a different source for each sound.

  1. Foley: Record 8 footsteps on a real surface with your phone or a recorder, peaks near -12 dB. Save the raw file.
  2. Library: Find a clean ambience or impact on Freesound. Record its URL, author, and license in a text file in your project.
  3. Synthesis: Make a UI confirmation blip using the Audacity tone steps above, or Bfxr.
  4. Keep all raw files unedited for now in a raw/ folder. The next lesson polishes them.

title: Editing Audio: Trim, Fades, Layering, and Pitch in a DAW
html:
From raw take to game-ready asset

Raw recordings are never ready to ship. They have silence at the start, room noise, inconsistent volume, and no punch. Editing turns a raw clip into a tight, reliable game asset. You can do all of it in a free tool like Audacity or any DAW (Reaper, Ableton, GarageBand, FL Studio). The five core operations are trim, normalize, fade, layer, and pitch.

1. Trim

The single most important edit. Game sounds must trigger instantly, so dead air at the start adds latency and makes feedback feel late. Zoom in on the waveform, find where the sound actually begins, and cut everything before it. Trim the tail too, leaving only as much as the natural decay needs.

Tip.
Always leave the very first sample a hair before the transient, but never trim into the attack. Cutting too tight clicks; cutting too loose feels laggy. Zero-crossing trims (cut where the wave crosses 0) avoid clicks.

2. Normalize / level

Bring each clip to a consistent peak (commonly -3 to -1 dB for one-shots) so your sounds are roughly balanced before they ever reach the engine. Use Effect > Normalize in Audacity. Remove steady background hiss with noise reduction: sample a silent region, then apply it gently. Heavy noise reduction sounds underwater, so use the lightest setting that works.

3. Fades

A fade is a gradual volume ramp. Tiny fades (5-20ms) at the start and end of a clip eliminate the click that happens when a waveform starts or stops mid-cycle. Longer fades shape character: a fast fade-in keeps a transient punchy, a slow fade-out makes a tail feel natural. Apply with Effect > Fade In / Fade Out.

4. Layering

Big game sounds are almost always several sounds stacked. A satisfying footstep might be a thud (the weight) plus a scuff (the surface) plus a tiny cloth rustle. Place each on its own track, line up their transients, and balance their levels. The classic recipe: a low layer for body, a mid layer for the main character, and a high layer for detail and definition.

layer a footstep
# Three tracks, transients aligned Track 1 (low) soft heel thud, EQ low-pass ~400Hz -6 dB Track 2 (mid) the foley step itself 0 dB Track 3 (high) gravel/cloth scuff, slight delay -9 dB # Mixdown > one punchy step. Repeat with different takes for variations.

5. Pitch

Pitch shifting transposes a sound up or down. Two big uses: tuning (a UI ding sounds nicer in tune with your music) and variation (shifting the same footstep by ±2-4 semitones gives free variety so repeats do not sound robotic). Use Effect > Change Pitch. Pitching down also adds weight and size; pitching up adds energy and smallness.

Lab note.
Generate variations programmatically at runtime too: many engines let you randomize pitch ±5% per playback. But baking a few distinct files plus runtime randomization gives the most natural result.

Export discipline

Export each finished sound as its own file with a clear name: footstep_grass_01.wav, footstep_grass_02.wav, ui_click.wav. Consistent naming saves hours when wiring into the engine. We choose formats and sample rates in the next lesson; for now export as 44.1kHz WAV.

🎮 Build This: Polish Your Palette

Turn last lesson's raw sounds into clean, punchy assets.

  1. Open your raw footstep recording. Split out 3 of the best individual steps.
  2. Trim each tight to the transient with a 10ms fade in and out. Normalize to about -2 dB.
  3. Layer in a low thud and a high scuff under one step, mix down to a punchy master step.
  4. Pitch-shift copies by -2 and +2 semitones to create footstep_01/02/03.wav.
  5. Trim and fade your UI blip, tune its pitch if needed, export as ui_click.wav.
  6. Export all files at 44.1kHz WAV with clear names.

title: Audio Formats and Optimization: WAV vs OGG, Mono vs Stereo, and Seamless Loops
html:
The format trade-off

Game audio balances quality, file size, CPU cost, and latency. The two formats you will use most are uncompressed WAV/PCM and compressed OGG Vorbis (or MP3/AAC on some platforms). The rule of thumb is simple:

Use caseFormatWhy
Short SFX, UI, footstepsWAV (PCM)Tiny anyway; no decode latency; can fire instantly and often
Music, long ambience, dialogueOGG / compressed10x smaller; the decode cost is worth it for long files

WAV is uncompressed, so a 2-second clip is small but a 3-minute track is huge. Compressed formats shrink that 10:1 but cost CPU to decode and add a little latency, which matters for a rapid-fire footstep but not for a 90-second loop. Engines often re-import to their own format anyway; you control this with import settings (Unity: Decompress on Load / Compressed in Memory / Streaming; Godot: Force Mono, loop, compression mode; Unreal: compression quality slider).

Mono vs stereo

This decision saves more memory than almost anything else. Mono = one channel; stereo = two (left and right). Critical rule: any sound that will be positioned in 3D must be mono. The engine places a mono source in space and computes the left/right balance itself based on listener position. A stereo source cannot be spatialized correctly. So:

  • Mono: footsteps, weapons, impacts, anything 3D-positioned, most SFX.
  • Stereo: music, 2D UI sounds, and wide ambience beds that are not positional.
Tip.
Importing a stereo footstep into a 3D game wastes double the memory and breaks attenuation. In Godot tick Force Mono; in Unity tick Force To Mono on the import settings.

Sample rate and bit depth

Sample rate is how many times per second the audio is measured. 44.1kHz (CD quality) is the standard for music and most SFX; 48kHz aligns with video. You rarely need higher. You can drop very dull sounds (a low rumble, a muffled thud) to 22kHz to save space since they have no high frequencies to lose. Keep 16-bit for delivery; 24-bit is for editing headroom only.

Seamless looping

A loop that clicks or stutters at the seam ruins immersion. Two things cause a bad loop: a discontinuity in the waveform at the loop point (click), and an audible event that does not line up (gap or bump). To author a seamless loop:

author a seamless ambience loop
1. Find a region with consistent texture (no unique one-off events). 2. Trim start AND end to zero-crossings to avoid clicks. 3. Crossfade the tail over the head: copy the last ~1s, fade it over the first ~1s so the seam blends. (Audacity: paste + Fade.) 4. Test by looping playback. Listen for the seam at the boundary. 5. Export as OGG, and set the import loop flag in the engine. # Avoid MP3 for loops: it can pad silence at the ends, breaking seams.
Watch out.
MP3 and some AAC encoders add tiny silent padding to the start/end of files, which creates an audible gap on every loop. Use OGG Vorbis or WAV for anything that loops.

Loudness across formats

Lossy compression slightly changes peaks; if you normalized to exactly 0 dB before encoding, the OGG can clip. Leave 1 dB of headroom (normalize to -1 dB) before exporting compressed audio.

🎮 Build This: Export the Right Way

Make correct format and channel choices for two different assets.

  1. Take a 30-60s ambience bed (wind, room tone). Author a seamless loop using the crossfade steps above.
  2. Normalize it to -1 dB, export as stereo OGG, and verify the seam is inaudible on repeat.
  3. Take your three footsteps and UI click. Export them as mono WAV, 44.1kHz, 16-bit.
  4. Write one sentence per file justifying its format and channel count.

title: Implementing Audio in Engines: Event Triggers, 3D Spatial Sound, and Buses
html:
From file to playing sound

An audio file in your project does nothing until something triggers it. Implementation is wiring sounds to game events, choosing whether they play in 2D or 3D space, and routing them through buses so you can mix them. The concepts are identical across engines; only the API names differ.

Triggering on events

You play a sound when a game event fires: a button is pressed, a foot plants, a bullet hits. Two patterns dominate. One-shots fire and forget (footstep, click). Looping sources start and stop with state (engine hum while driving, ambience while in a zone). For footsteps, the cleanest trigger is the animation system: an animation event on the frame the foot touches the ground. Simpler prototypes trigger on a timer while moving.

trigger a footstep with pitch variation (Godot 4)
# AudioStreamPlayer3D on the player, footsteps[] = your 3 WAVs func play_footstep(): $Step.stream = footsteps[randi() % footsteps.size()] $Step.pitch_scale = randf_range(0.95, 1.05) # subtle variety $Step.play()
UI click on a button (Unity C#)
// AudioSource set to 2D (Spatial Blend = 0), assigned to UI bus public AudioSource uiSource; public AudioClip click; // Hook OnClick in the Button inspector: public void OnButtonClick() { uiSource.PlayOneShot(click); }

2D vs 3D spatial audio

2D sound plays at a fixed left/right balance regardless of where things are; it is for UI and music. 3D sound is positioned in the world: the engine pans and attenuates it based on the listener (usually the camera or player). Footsteps, weapons, and world objects should be 3D so players can locate them by ear.

Engine2D control3D control
UnitySpatial Blend = 0Spatial Blend = 1, set rolloff curve
GodotAudioStreamPlayer (2D)AudioStreamPlayer3D + Attenuation
UnrealNo attenuation assetAttenuation Settings asset

Attenuation

Attenuation is how a 3D sound fades with distance. You set a min distance (full volume inside this radius) and a max distance (silent beyond it), plus a falloff curve. Logarithmic rolloff is realistic; linear is simpler and easier to tune for gameplay readability. Set the max distance so distant sounds do not clutter the mix but important nearby ones stay clear.

Tip.
One listener only. Make sure exactly one AudioListener (Unity) / Listener / Camera (Godot, Unreal) is active, usually on the camera or player. Two listeners cause doubled, phasey audio.

Buses and mixing

A bus (or mixer group) is a channel that many sounds route into, so you can control them as a group. Set up a bus per layer: Master → (Music, SFX, UI, Ambience). Now a single SFX volume slider in your options menu controls every gameplay sound at once, and you can apply effects (reverb, EQ, compression) to a whole group. In Godot you add buses in the Audio panel and set each player's Bus property; in Unity you create groups in an AudioMixer and assign Output; in Unreal you use Submixes and Sound Classes.

Lab note.
Always route through buses from day one, even with three sounds. Retrofitting bus routing late is tedious, and you will want it the moment you build an options menu or set up ducking.
🎮 Build This: Wire the SFX

Get your edited sounds playing in an engine with proper routing.

  1. Create buses: Master with child buses Music, SFX, UI, Ambience.
  2. Add a 3D audio source to your player; assign your 3 footstep WAVs, route to the SFX bus. Trigger one per step (animation event or movement timer) with random selection and ±5% pitch.
  3. Set attenuation: min distance ~1m, max ~20m, so a distant character's steps fade out.
  4. Add a 2D audio source on a UI button; play ui_click.wav on press, routed to the UI bus.
  5. Walk toward and away from a sound source and confirm the footsteps attenuate and pan correctly.

title: Composing Simple Game Music: Loops, Motifs, and Tension and Release
html:
Music that survives repetition

Game music is different from a song. A song plays once; a game loop plays for hours. The central challenge of composing for games is writing music that does not fatigue when repeated. That changes how you write: simpler, more spacious, motif-driven, and arranged to support gameplay rather than demand attention.

You do not need to be a trained composer

A DAW (GarageBand, Reaper, LMMS, FL Studio) plus free instrument plugins is enough. You will work with a MIDI piano roll, drawing notes on a grid, so you do not need to play an instrument. The musical building blocks are few: pick a key, build chords from its scale, write a motif, and arrange.

Motif: the memorable hook

A motif is a short, recognizable musical idea, often just 3-5 notes. Think of the most famous game themes; their hooks are tiny. A motif gives a loop identity and lets you create variation by transforming the same idea (move it up, change its rhythm, harmonize it) instead of writing endless new material. Write one short motif and reuse it. Repetition with variation feels intentional; random new melodies feel noisy.

Harmony, tension, and release

Music creates emotion by building tension and then releasing it. The simplest engine for this is a chord progression that moves away from the home chord (tonic) and returns. A reliable, pleasant loop in a minor key:

a calm loopable progression (A minor)
# 4 bars, one chord per bar, ~80 BPM Am (home / rest) → F (lift, warmth) C (brightness) → G (tension, wants to resolve) # G pulls back to Am, so the loop point feels natural, not abrupt. Tip: end the loop on G so returning to Am at bar 1 = release.

The trick for a seamless musical loop: arrange so the last chord wants to move to the first chord. The harmonic pull across the loop seam makes the repeat feel like a continuation rather than a restart.

Arrangement for low fatigue

  • Leave space. Constant busy melody tires the ear fast. Let the motif breathe; use sustained pads and gaps.
  • Limit the high-attention layer. A prominent lead melody draws focus. For background loops, keep melody sparse and lean on harmony and texture.
  • Match the gameplay. Exploration music is calm and slow; combat is driving and dense. Tempo and density carry mood more than melody.
  • Keep loops longer than they feel. A 30-60 second loop fatigues far less than an 8-second one, even though both repeat.
Tip.
Compose to a grid and fixed tempo from the start. Bar-aligned, tempo-locked music is essential for the adaptive layering and crossfading you will build next lesson.

Mixing the loop

Keep the music mix simple: bass, a pad/harmony, a light melody, and optionally percussion. Pan layers slightly for width, leave headroom (peaks around -6 dB so SFX have room to sit on top), and export as a seamless loop using the techniques from Lesson 8.4. Music is usually stereo and OGG.

Lab note.
If you cannot write a melody, a loop can be just a bass line plus a pad plus rhythmic texture. Atmospheric exploration music often has no real melody at all, which also makes it less fatiguing.
🎮 Build This: A Loop With a Motif

Compose a short, seamless background loop.

  1. In a DAW, set a tempo (e.g. 80 BPM) and pick a minor key.
  2. Lay down a 4-bar chord progression that ends wanting to return to the home chord (e.g. Am-F-C-G).
  3. Write a 3-5 note motif over it; keep it sparse and leave space.
  4. Add a bass line following the chord roots and a soft pad for warmth.
  5. Make it loop: ensure the end leads musically into the start, and that any reverb tail does not cut off abruptly.
  6. Mix with peaks around -6 dB, export as a seamless stereo OGG. Loop it for 5 minutes and check that it does not grate.

title: Adaptive Music and Middleware: Vertical Layering, Horizontal Re-sequencing, FMOD vs Wwise
html:
Music that responds to the game

A static loop ignores what the player is doing. Adaptive (or dynamic) music changes with the game state: it intensifies in combat, calms when you explore, swells at a discovery. There are two fundamental techniques, and most games use both.

Vertical layering (re-orchestration)

You compose multiple layers that all play in perfect sync on the same timeline and the same chord progression. You then change the music by fading layers in and out. A calm scene plays just the pad and bass; as danger rises you fade in a percussion layer, then a lead/strings layer. Because all layers share tempo and harmony, they can be mixed in any combination at any moment without a transition point.

vertical layers, one shared 60s timeline
Layer A (base) pad + bass always on Layer B (tension) percussion + arp fade in at intensity > 0.5 Layer C (combat) lead + heavy drums fade in at intensity > 0.8 # All three loop in sync. Crossfade volumes, never restart playback.

Horizontal re-sequencing

Here you have distinct music segments (explore, combat, victory) and you switch between them over time. The key problem is the transition: jumping mid-bar sounds jarring. Solutions include waiting for the next bar or beat to switch (quantized transitions), and using short transition stingers to bridge segments. Horizontal works well for distinct gameplay phases; vertical works well for a continuously variable intensity.

Vertical layeringHorizontal re-sequencing
ChangesDensity / orchestrationWhich section plays
Best forContinuous intensity (a threat meter)Discrete states (explore vs boss)
TransitionInstant crossfade, always in syncNeeds quantization or stingers

Middleware vs engine-native

Middleware is dedicated audio software that sits between your sounds and the engine. The two industry standards are FMOD and Wwise. Both let sound designers build adaptive behavior (layers, transitions, randomization, real-time parameters, mixing) in their own authoring tool, then the programmer just sends simple events and parameters from the game. This decouples audio work from code.

OptionStrengthsConsider when
Engine-native (Unity/Godot/Unreal audio)No extra integration; free; fine for simple needsSmall projects, basic layering, learning
FMODApproachable, great for music systems, free for small budgetsIndie to mid-size, music-heavy games
WwiseDeep, powerful profiler, huge AAA adoptionLarge projects, big SFX counts, complex mixing
Tip.
For your first adaptive system, engine-native is perfect. The concepts (synced layers, crossfading, parameters) transfer directly to FMOD/Wwise later. Do not adopt middleware until you feel native audio limiting you.

Building vertical layering natively

The implementation is straightforward. Start both layers playing at the same time (so they stay sample-synced) and animate their volumes with an intensity value. Never start a layer late, or it will be out of phase.

2-layer crossfade by intensity (Godot 4)
# Two AudioStreamPlayers, both .play() at start, both looping in sync func set_intensity(t: float): # t = 0.0 calm .. 1.0 intense $Base.volume_db = linear_to_db(1.0) # base always full $Layer.volume_db = linear_to_db(clamp(t, 0, 1)) # fade in tension # Smooth t over ~1s with lerp so the change is a crossfade, not a jump.
🎮 Build This: 2-Layer Adaptive Loop

Turn your composition into a system that responds to game state.

  1. From last lesson's loop, bounce two synced versions on the same timeline: a base (pad + bass) and an intensity layer (add percussion + lead). Same tempo, length, and loop point.
  2. Export both as seamless OGG loops.
  3. In the engine, load both into separate audio sources on the Music bus. Start both playing and looping at the same moment.
  4. Add an intensity variable (0-1). Map it to the intensity layer's volume; keep the base at full.
  5. Smoothly lerp intensity over ~1 second so transitions crossfade. Bind it to a key or a proximity trigger (near enemy = 1, far = 0).
  6. Test: the music should thicken near danger and thin out when safe, with no restart or sync drift.

title: Mixing for Games: Ducking, Loudness, and the Final Soundscape
html:
The mix is where it all comes together

You can have great sounds and still have a bad soundscape if the mix is wrong: music drowning footsteps, a UI click that pierces, ambience so loud nobody hears dialogue. Mixing for games is balancing every layer so the most important sound at any moment is always audible, and the whole output sits at a comfortable, consistent loudness.

Set a level hierarchy

Decide what matters most and build the mix around it. A common priority order: dialogue/critical cues > key SFX (the player's own actions) > music > ambience > minor SFX. Set your bus levels to reflect that. Music in particular is almost always too loud in first drafts; pull it back until SFX read clearly.

BusTypical relationship
SFX (player actions)Reference level, the loudest gameplay element
MusicSits under SFX; ducks when key sounds play
AmbienceQuiet bed, felt more than heard
UIClear but not piercing

Ducking: making room automatically

Ducking lowers one bus when another plays, so important sounds cut through. The classic use is ducking music and ambience under dialogue or under loud SFX. It is implemented with a sidechain compressor: the dialogue bus signal triggers volume reduction on the music bus. When dialogue stops, music returns. This keeps music full when it can be, but gets out of the way the instant something important happens.

sidechain duck: music under dialogue
# On the Music bus, add a Compressor in sidechain mode: Sidechain input = Dialogue bus Threshold set so dialogue triggers it Ratio 4:1 (amount of duck) Attack ~20ms (fast, so it ducks promptly) Release ~300ms (smooth return after dialogue) # Unity: AudioMixer + Duck Volume effect. FMOD/Wwise: sidechain/ducking built in.
Tip.
No dialogue in your game? Duck music slightly under important SFX (hits, pickups, alerts) instead. A small, fast duck makes every key action pop without you turning the music down permanently.

Loudness: the target that matters

Loudness is not peak level; it is perceived volume measured in LUFS (Loudness Units Full Scale). Mixing to a consistent integrated loudness keeps players from constantly reaching for the volume knob and meets platform requirements. Useful targets:

  • -14 LUFS integrated is a common, sensible target for game output (also the streaming norm).
  • Keep true peak below -1 dBTP to avoid clipping after compression/encoding.
  • Preserve dynamic range: games are not loudness-war pop tracks. A quiet exploration scene should be quieter than combat. Do not crush everything to maximum.
Watch out.
Do not mix loud. Set a comfortable monitoring volume and leave it. If you mix at high volume, everything sounds good and balanced; played back quietly it falls apart. Mix at a moderate, fixed level and check on different devices (headphones, laptop speakers, phone).

Metering and final delivery

Put a loudness meter on the master bus (free options exist for every DAW; engines and middleware have built-in meters too). Play through representative gameplay and watch the integrated LUFS over time. Adjust the master so typical gameplay sits near your target, then verify true peak stays under -1 dBTP. Finally, do a mono check: collapse to mono and confirm nothing important disappears (phase cancellation between stereo layers can erase sounds in mono playback on some devices).

Lab note.
Add a brick-wall limiter as the last effect on the master to catch stray peaks, but use it as a safety net, not as a way to make the whole game louder. The limiter should barely engage during normal play.
🎮 Build This: Final Mix Pass

Mix your full prototype soundscape into a cohesive whole.

  1. With footsteps, UI click, ambience loop, and 2-layer adaptive music all running, set bus levels: SFX clear and present, music under it, ambience as a quiet bed.
  2. Add ducking: a fast, light duck on the Music bus triggered by key SFX (or dialogue if you have it).
  3. Put a loudness meter on the master. Play through calm and intense states; aim for roughly -14 LUFS integrated.
  4. Add a limiter on the master with a ceiling of -1 dBTP as a safety net.
  5. Check the mix on headphones, laptop speakers, and in mono. Confirm footsteps stay audible during music, and nothing clips or vanishes.
  6. Adjust until the important sound is always readable in every game state.
